weather: Today in Edmonds: Mostly sunny to partly sunny, high 67°F (feeling comfortable). Currently 52°F with light north winds 2-5 mph. Precipitation chance near zero. Evening turns partly cloudy with low around 50°F and calm winds. Perfect spring day for outdoor activities with Piper.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council on April 28 unanimously adopted the city’s first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan, a $300,000 roadmap that identifies 11 priority traffic-safety projects eligible for federal Safe Streets and Roads for All grants. Projects range from downtown-core intersection fixes to speed-calming along Highway 99 segments. Council also approved placing a $383,456 amendment on a future consent agenda to fund Phase 2 of the Perrinville Creek flow-reduction study, a joint effort with Lynnwood to curb chronic flooding.

During the same April 28 session, council received annual workload reports from the city’s contract prosecutor (Walls Law Group) and the Snohomish County Public Defender Association. Prosecutor Eric Walls reported hiring an additional attorney has eliminated last year’s case backlog, while Public Defender Kathleen Kyle noted misdemeanor theft—median value $37.66—remains the top charge in Edmonds courts.

The 22nd annual Puget Sound Bird Fest will return to Edmonds June 6–7, co-hosted by the Pilchuck Audubon Society and the city. Events include guided bird walks, a seabird cruise from Edmonds Marina, native-plant sale, kids’ activities at the library, and a “Every Bird Has a Song” theme focused on avian vocalizations. Registration for walks and a photography workshop opens Friday.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica: 2026-27 season auditions will be held in August 2026—email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list. Requirements include vocalizing, sight-reading two short choral pieces, repeating a phrase by ear, and preparing a two-minute solo. Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30–9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Dues $75/year with scholarships available. Seattle Choral Company: auditions for the 2025-26 season took place in September 2025; next cycle expected late summer 2026. Choral Arts Northwest: free Music Educator Sing-in on 15 March 2026 at First United Methodist Church, Queen Anne—registration open at choralartsnw.org.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries toddler storytimes in May 2026: Edmonds Library—Toddler Storytime with Cami every Wednesday 9:00-9:45 am. Lynnwood Library—Toddler Storytime with Nathalie Mondays 9:30-10:00 am and Fridays 10:30-11:00 am. Both libraries also offer Baby Storytimes and Family Storytimes; check sno-isle.bibliocommons.com for full calendars. Imagine Children’s Museum (Everett) hosts toddler craft sessions such as bee & butterfly headband making and giant flower mural painting on Saturday 9 May, 10 am & 12 pm.

global_news.findings: China has announced a sweeping ban on drone sales in Beijing, citing security concerns. The new rules, effective Friday, prohibit the sale, rental, or import of drones and key components into the capital. Drone owners must register devices with police, and all outdoor flights require prior approval and online training. This move could significantly impact China's massive drone market, which is expected to generate over $290 billion by 2035, as Beijing becomes one of the most difficult places to fly drones despite being home to major manufacturers like DJI.

Israeli naval forces intercepted a 22-boat Gaza aid flotilla in international waters near Crete, detaining approximately 175 pro-Palestinian activists. The Global Sumud Flotilla, which set sail from Europe to break Israel's Gaza blockade, was intercepted 600 miles from Gaza. Israel claims the operation was lawful and peaceful, while organizers denounce it as 'piracy.' The activists will be disembarked on a Greek beach, with Italy demanding the release of 24 detained Italians and the EU calling for respect of international law.

The Kremlin confirmed Russian forces will remain in Mali to combat extremism, despite recent setbacks where al Qaeda-linked insurgents and Tuareg separatists killed the defense minister and forced Russian contractors to withdraw from strategic towns. The al Qaeda affiliate Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin claims to have captured additional territory, including the Hombori military base, and is threatening to besiege the capital Bamako. This highlights Russia's strategic challenges in Africa as its security guarantor image faces pressure.

AI-generated images falsely linking Washington assassination attempt suspect Cole Tomas Allen to British public figures including Rishi Sunak and Nigel Farage have been circulating online. Reuters Fact Check confirmed these images, which show Allen posing with various celebrities, were created using Google's AI tools. The images falsely claim Allen worked as a driver for these figures, highlighting the growing challenge of AI-generated misinformation following high-profile incidents.

The EU approved a €90 billion loan for Ukraine as the Druzhba oil pipeline resumed operations, ending months of deadlock over stalled oil supplies to Hungary. This financial package comes as Ukraine continues to adapt its economy to wartime conditions while seeking technological support from European allies. The pipeline restart represents a significant breakthrough in resolving regional energy disputes amid ongoing conflict.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica investigation reveals that more than 300 U.S. citizens and lawful protesters have been arrested by federal agents during President Trump’s immigration sweeps, yet the cases collapse under scrutiny.  Officers repeatedly claimed protesters assaulted or interfered with law-enforcement, but body-camera and bystander video disproved those statements; over one-third of the cases have already been dismissed or lost at trial.  The pattern—mass arrests followed by rapid collapse—has created a chilling effect: activists say they now avoid demonstrations for fear of prison, even whe [TRUNCATED]

In a second major feature, ProPublica details how the Trump administration has spent the past fifteen months systematically dismantling the federal guardrails that prevented the overturning of the 2020 election.  At least 75 career civil servants have been pushed out of the Departments of Justice, Homeland Security and Defense, while two dozen new political appointees—nearly half of whom publicly denied the 2020 result—have been installed.  The story reconstructs a previously unreported December 2020 meeting in which Attorney General William Barr summoned cyber-security experts and FBI officia [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s long essay on Frances ‘Fanny’ Wright portrays the nineteenth-century Scottish-American reformer as a forgotten foremother of American scientific thinking and radical feminism.  Raised on the works of French materialists and tutored by a Glasgow moral-philosophy professor, Wright published ‘A Few Days in Athens’ at eighteen—a philosophical novel that recast Epicurean atomism as a moral system predicated on justice and tolerance.  Popova traces how Wright’s itinerant lectures on education, abolition and gender equality captivated a young Walt Whitman, who recalled that she ‘posses [TRUNCATED]

The 23 April 2026 issue of the LRB carries Oliver Cussen’s essay ‘History after Climate Change,’ which revisits Dipesh Chakrabarty’s influential argument that anthropogenic climate change undermines the basic categories—agency, experience, causation—on which conventional historiography depends.  Cussen extends the critique, suggesting that the Anthropocene forces historians to abandon the long-standing distinction between natural and human history, and to confront the possibility that the archive itself may become illegible as sea-level rise and extreme weather threaten physical collections.   [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three recent works advance triadic-relational metaphysics: a 2025 Karl Alber volume “Trinitarian Ontologies” (Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy, Book 1) collects interdisciplinary essays bridging analytic and continental approaches to trinitarian thought; Damiano Migliorini’s 2025 monograph “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics” constructs a rigorous analytic model that replaces substance metaphysics with relational subsistences, events and ‘gunk’ to render the Trinity coherent; and Christopher Isabelle’s 2026-targeted “Triadic Relational Ontology” paper argues that dyadic structures are insufficient for emergence—only a maintained triadic relational field can sustain intelligibility after primordial separation.

uap.findings: The search returned three key documents: the official 13 Nov 2024 House joint hearing "Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Exposing the Truth" featuring Luis Elizondo and other witnesses; an April 2026 press release from Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s Task Force demanding UAP videos from the Department of War and citing whistleblower claims; and a September 2025 UPI report on a dramatic Oversight subcommittee hearing where veterans described triangle-shaped craft and a video of a missile bouncing off an orb, all underscoring ongoing congressional pressure for transparency on non-human intelligence.

newyorker.title: Reverend Billy Takes On Norman Foster’s New Monolith
newyorker.dek: Fresh from opening shows for Neil Young, the street preacher Billy Talen has moved on from burning Mickey Mouse in effigy to protesting JPMorgan Chase’s ties to fossil fuels, Jake Offenhartz writes.
newyorker.text:
A little after midnight on Earth Day, the street preacher Billy Talen dragged his pulpit to the new Norman Foster-designed global headquarters of JPMorgan Chase, on Madison. His self-ordained mission: deliver a twenty-four-hour sermon, calling attention to the bank’s ties to the fossil-fuel industry. Talen, who is seventy-five, held an umbrella above his white leisure suit and silver bouffant, eying a pair of bank security guards. “Blessings upon your souls, gentlemen,” he bellowed. Members of his activist ensemble, the Stop Shopping Choir, were setting up a live stream in the rain. Savitri Durkee, who directs both the choir and Talen, who is her husband, clipped a mike to his lapel and checked her watch. “It’s go-time, Billy,” she said. “See you on the other side.”
Talen, who goes by Reverend Billy, is fond of what he calls “extreme preaching.” He began in the mid-nineties, marching around Times Square with “Antichrist” Mickey Mouse nailed to a wooden cross, in protest of the neighborhood’s creeping corporatism. In the years since, his focus has shifted to the climate crisis. Often, this involves staging musical protests in hostile venues—banks, pipeline-construction zones—until he and his flock are arrested for trespassing. But, Talen said, even that has begun to feel “predictable.” He elaborated, speaking into a camera: “The only way we’re going to start an effective movement is if each of us goes to the limit of our capabilities and claims our eccentricities. I’m a preacher, so I’m going to preach until I drop.” A choir member checked the stream, and noted that they’d just jumped from five to fourteen viewers. “Earthalujah!” Talen cried.
After decades of sneaking up on bankers and lobbyists, Reverend Billy and the choir recently booked a straight gig: touring the country as Neil Young’s opening act. (Typical song: “A Tree Is the Opposite of a Cop.”) The rock star’s fans seemed receptive to the message, relatively. “After a lifetime of being too strange, people are starting to get us,” Talen said. He’s also the religion editor for the Times-Contrarian, Young’s anti-corporate “digital newspaper.” “Neil is a hands-on publisher,” Talen said. “Sometimes he lets me write these really long things. Other times, he’ll say to me, ‘Billy, what do you mean here? What do you want to leave us with?,’ and we’ll e-mail back and forth on that.” Talen said that he’s learned a lot from Young as a performer: “He’s taught me to hold the rhythm, hit the rhyme. He gets what appeals to a mass audience.” Talen and Durkee were connected with the singer by Young’s wife, Daryl Hannah, whom they met at a 2009 protest against mountaintop-removal coal mining in West Virginia. Talen was later arrested, after depositing mounds of “sacred dirt”—taken from the mountain, he claimed—at two Chase branches in the East Village. The bank has since decreased financing for companies involved in mountaintop-removal mining.
By 8 A.M., dark circles had formed under Talen’s steely blue eyes. “I just need to stay hydrated,” he said. Behind him, a gardener was washing a living-wall installation titled “A Parallel Nature,” designed for Chase by Maya Lin. “I’m finding myself mesmerized by this strange, sculpted nature wall,” Talen said. “It feels like a memory.” After conferring with a guard, the gardener pointed his hose at the protesters in the plaza. Talen pulled a rain slicker over his suit and glowered. Thousands of workers were now streaming into the building, mostly ignoring the doomsday preacher.
Around lunchtime, roughly two dozen choir members and other activists arrived for a 5K run around the bank. Among them was Ahmed, an asylum seeker from Guinea who had taken refuge in the choir’s former headquarters, a vacant bank on Avenue C, after arriving in the United States. “It’s very scary-looking,” he said of Chase’s sixty-story tower. Many of the runners had procured thrift-store blazers or top hats, an effort to blend in with the bankers. Durkee, who wore a pin-striped suit, led the group in some stretches, followed by twelve laps around the block. A construction worker sanding down a railing offered support—“You got this!”—but appeared confused by a runner’s sign accusing Chase of ecocide. “I thought it was, like, a charity thing,” he said.
As night fell, Talen huddled under a blanket with other choir members. His once thunderous voice had dropped to a rasp as he condemned the “gangsters in suits” responsible for rising global temperatures. In the final minutes of his sermon, he found himself at an unusual loss for words. “It feels like, well, extreme exhaustion, I guess,” he said. “Or a long hike that got out of hand, and now I have to come down the mountain.” Had the suffering been worth it? “I’m not sure we cracked the culture,” he said. “But we had a vision, and we did what we could.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
literary_pick.author: Rebecca Skloot
literary_pick.summary: A groundbreaking work of narrative nonfiction that tells the story of Henrietta Lacks, a poor Black tobacco farmer whose cells—taken without her knowledge in 1951—became one of the most important tools in medicine, vital for developing the polio vaccine, cloning, gene mapping, and more. Skloot's book masterfully weaves together the history of medical experimentation on African Americans, the birth of bioethics, and the legal battles over who owns our bodies, while exploring the profound impact on Lacks's family who remained in poverty despite the billions made from her cells.

enriched_articles.title: Russia confirms its forces will remain in Mali to combat extremism despite escalating attacks
enriched_articles.text: Russia confirms its forces will remain in Mali to combat extremism despite escalating attacks. The Kremlin announced that Russian military forces will continue their presence in Mali to fight what he called "extremism and terrorism" within the country. He added that Russia considers its mission part of supporting stability in the region, as extremist attacks in Mali have increased significantly in recent months.

enriched_articles.title: President Trump says 'a lot of things' are coming in UFO files
enriched_articles.text: President Donald Trump said his administration will release "a lot of things" about unidentified flying objects to the American public in the near future. Trump said during a White House event that he interviewed people who told him that "they saw things you wouldn't believe." The president ordered the Defense Department and other agencies to release government documents related to UFOs in February, including files on "alien and extraterrestrial life, unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP), and uni

enriched_articles.title: CES 2026: Motorola is entering the wearable AI game
enriched_articles.text: Motorola debuted a new wearable AI proof of concept at CES 2026 during its Lenovo World Tour event. The device is powered by Motorola's new AI assistant, Qira, and can identify and explain objects in front of you, summarize information, and even navigate to events by launching apps on a paired phone. Motorola was clear that this wearable is still a long way from any commercial release.

enriched_articles.title: Études in Light and Harmony: An interdisciplinary workbook for creative dialogue and discovery
enriched_articles.text: An interdisciplinary workbook exploring foundational questions in physics through a Judeo-Christian lens. The text develops a "logic of three" to overcome dualities and explores how light, life, and word converge through return as a generating trope of identity and difference. It examines relationships between physics, metaphysics, theology, and other disciplines through triadic logic and relational ontology.

enriched_articles.title: Contents · Vol. 48 No. 5 · 19 March 2026
enriched_articles.text: The March 19, 2026 issue of London Review of Books features essays including "Who owns the Arctic?" by Laleh Khalili examining historical claims to Arctic territories, "Politics on Speed" by William Davies on post-Brexit political dynamics, and "De Kooning in Cuba" by T.J. Clark analyzing the artist's work during revolutionary periods. The issue covers literature, politics, and cultural criticism.
